does: ConceptViz presents a visual analytics system that bridges sparse autoencoder (SAE) features in large language models with human-interpretable concepts. The system implements a three-stage pipeline—identification, interpretation, and validation—that allows users to query SAEs using concepts of interest, interactively explore alignments between concepts and learned features, and verify these correspondences through model behavior analysis. The work demonstrates the system's effectiveness through two usage scenarios and a user study, showing that ConceptViz reduces the labor-intensive manual interpretation process that has hampered SAE-based mechanistic interpretability research.
connects: This work builds on the emerging line of research in mechanistic interpretability that seeks to understand how neural networks internally represent information. Sparse autoencoders have recently become a key technique for extracting interpretable features from LLM activations, extending prior work on dictionary learning and neural feature decomposition. However, the gap between SAE-extracted features and human concepts represents an open challenge in interpretability—prior approaches relied on manual, labor-intensive interpretation. ConceptViz responds to this limitation by combining SAE feature extraction with interactive visualization and concept-based querying, drawing on visual analytics methodology to make the interpretation process more systematic and scalable than previous ad-hoc approaches.
enables: ConceptViz enables a more systematic and scalable approach to mechanistic interpretability research by automating the labor-intensive process of connecting learned features to human concepts. This foundation could support rapid discovery of how LLMs encode specific domains of knowledge, from reasoning processes to factual representations. The system's interactive validation pipeline could accelerate research into steering or controlling model behavior through concept-based interventions, and may pave the way for automated concept discovery and hierarchical concept mapping. The work also points toward building better interpretability tools that could eventually support broader model auditing, bias detection, and safety verification efforts by making LLM internals more transparent to researchers and practitioners.
